entry.title: Change company code currency
entry.content:
Is it possible to change the company code currency?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Change company code currency
entry.content:
Company Code currency is local currency of a country. It is predefined and cannot be changed in S/4HANA Public Cloud

See community post

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/freely-defined-currency-or-additional-currency-functional-currency-in-s/ba-p/13564681

   
 See help.sap.com documentation on Currencies https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/f22f2aca75404b5f84becb9b246023b2.html?locale=en-US 
   

See also 
Change of Functional Currency https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/73c10be5deac49c993956ab3281c9ea1.html?locale=en-US

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/usd-currency-as-local-currency-for-singapore-company-code-in-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/13563002 

2812802 - Request to change the company code currency from local currency to other currency

3492731 - FAQ: Currency Translation in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud

entry.title: Commerce Automation with Non-SAP ERP Commodity codes
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

Trying to understand the best possible ways to handle the commodity code (Taxonomy) in Ariba Invoice Management. We are implementing Commerce Automation and Ariba Invoice Management for a Non-SAP ERP backend, as part of this, we have a list of custom commodity codes that are used as Product groups in non-SAP ERP. I am aware of the 4 commodity code files (commodity export, Classification code map, ERP Commodity codes/Partition commodity code and CEME). But in our case, since the POs will be initiated from Non-SAP ERP System, when the invoices reach the Network, we expect the suppliers to submit invoices over the network. When suppliers submit the invoices over the network, it should flow to Ariba Invoice Management solution, and we should be able to capture the Commodity code details from the PO to Invoice Reconciliation document which will drive the accounting combination in Ariba Invoice Management as per the CEME (Commodity Export Map Export) combination.

From the above scenario, when the PO is created in Non-SAP ERP, we will not have the Commodity code (UNSPSC) captured anywhere on the PO Output to Network, but we may be able to send the ERP Commodity code / ERP Product group code. But when an invoice is submitted via network, will the Commodity code be determined automatically based on the ERP Product group code in Ariba Invoice Management?

Am looking for the best practices in this case on how we can leverage the Commodity code files in SAP Ariba Invoice Management while connecting with Non-SAP ERP System.

Please note that, since backend system is Non-SAP ERP, the Ariba Invoice Management solution is a Generic Variant provided to us and hence CIG is Out of scope, and we have BTP as middleware.

Any help or suggestions or how you have handled the above scenarios while enabling commerce Automation for PO Collaboration and Ariba Invoice Management for a Non-SAP ERP would be very much appreciated.

Thanks,
Velu

entry.title: Integrating SAP Conversational A.I with SAP Analytics Cloud - Powered by GEN A.I & Intercom
entry.content:
👋Introduction :

The lack of integrated support facilities within SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) creates a functional shortcoming, as business users must rely on human support teams for basic queries and troubleshooting. This results in delays, increased support workload, and inefficiencies, limiting users' ability to fully leverage SAC for real-time insights and decision-making.

🚀Bridging the Support Gap - How SAP Conversational AI Transforms User Experience in SAC :

To address this functional shortcoming, integrating SAP Conversational AI into SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) provides a powerful solution. The AI bot offers instant, automated support for business users, answering FAQs directly within SAC dashboards and reports. This reduces the need for human intervention, streamlining workflows, improving decision-making, and enhancing overall productivity. When the bot encounters more complex queries, it can seamlessly escalate to ChatGPT or route users to live support, ensuring comprehensive, efficient assistance at all stages of the user experience.

✔️Prerequisites for Integrating SAP Conversational AI Bot with SAC:

  • ✔️SAP Analytics Cloud & SAP Conversational AI account
  • ✔️FAQ Knowledge base
  • ✔️Chat GPT - API Key (Paid). Open-source Models are available for free.
  • ✔️Intercom Account  - (Free trial available)
  • ✔️Active Internet (kidding 😁) 

🚀Let's get started with Bot 🤖 :

⚠️ I've combined all of my snapshots into spoiler tags to make my blog more engaging and less scrollable. To view the Images, expand the Spoiler tab ⚠️

Given your familiarity with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), we will now proceed to explore the process of building a bot using SAP Conversational AI.

🏗High-Level System Architecture🏗:

⚙️Configuring an FAQ Bot🤖:

1. On the home page please go with the New Bot button.

2. Select the FAQ Bot from the available options.

Spoiler

3. Name your bot, enter the required details, and click 'Create'.

4. You will be directed to your bot's homepage, where you can train it and add additional features.

Spoiler

5. Under the Train tab, upload a CSV file to serve as your knowledge base. Column A should contain the questions, while Column B should contain the corresponding answers. Please ensure the file is saved in CSV format, as XLSX is unsupported. For your reference, I have attached a sample question bank below.

Spoiler

 

6. Once your file has been submitted, you can use the online interface to modify or add new questions and answers. There is no need to upload the file again.

7. This part is where you may add additional questions or alternative terminology.

Spoiler

8. You may maintain your knowledge Base under the Train tab, and you can train your bot with four skills under the Build tab.

Spoiler
Fig1
9. In the connect tab, you can choose how to integrate your chatbot with your application, in our example, SAP Analytics Cloud.
Spoiler

10. After clicking on SAP Conversational AI Web Client, complete the basic setup by selecting your bot's theme, specifying the duration for saving conversation history, entering your channel name, and clicking proceed.

Spoiler
11. We are only able to use the standalone link, as the HTML script cannot be utilized within SAP Analytics Cloud. You may use the URL to view your bot in a web browser.
Spoiler
12. The Fallback Channels subtab under the Connect tab allows you to integrate Intercom for live chat with a support executive.
Spoiler

13. After creating a free trial account with Intercom, you can now access the Developer Hub and create a new app.

Spoiler
14. Once you have created your app in Intercom, you will gain access to the access tokens.
Spoiler

15. After copying your access token, return to SAP Conversational AI, navigate to the Connect tab, and select the Fallback Channel option. Choose Intercom, paste your access token, and click OK. This will generate a webhook URL. Follow the on-screen instructions to configure the webhook in the Intercom Hub.

Once these steps are completed, the integration with Intercom will be established, allowing you to proceed with the ChatGPT integration.

Spoiler

🔗Integrating and Setting Up the ChatGPT API (Paid)

1. Visit the OpenAI website and create an account at https://openai.com/api/.

2. Go to your profile, and on the left side, you will find the "API Keys" option. Select it to generate your API key.

Spoiler

3. Follow the documentation to test your API in Postman. https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/chat/create

Since the ChatGPT API is no longer free, I am now using Groq AI, which offers functionality similar to ChatGPT but is based on the META LLaMA 3 model, the same model used by META AI. You can explore more at https://groq.com/.

🔍We can now explore how to utilize the Gen A.I Model call in our FAQ skill:

The FAQ bot operates based on a confidence score, which is determined by the relevance of the question to the knowledge base. If the question asked closely matches an entry in the knowledge base, the confidence score will be high. If the relevance is moderate, the confidence score will be medium. In cases where the question is not relevant to any entry in the knowledge base, the confidence score will be very low.

For scenarios where the confidence score is very low (e.g., less than or equal to 0.05), we will configure the bot to trigger a GPT call, allowing it to provide an answer from the LLMs.

1. When the FAQ Skill is opened, you may observe conditions for high-confidence

Spoiler

2. The bot will retrieve relevant questions from the knowledge base and prompt the user to select the appropriate question if the confidence score falls between 0.06 and 0.9.

Spoiler

3. If the confidence score is 0.05 or lower, we will initiate an API request to ChatGPT or another generative AI model.

The (+) icon allows you to select different types of messages. For example, I have chosen a text message to notify the user that the query is outside of our knowledge base and that a generative AI model will be used to provide the answer. As shown in the image below, you can also set a timer between messages for better flow.

Spoiler

4. Since our GPT callback will use REST API services, select "Connect to External Service" and then "Consume API Service" after notifying the user.

Spoiler
Spoiler

5. Enter the chat completion URL from the ChatGPT documentation into the POST URL field. In this case, I have used the Groq API URL. Then, paste the authentication token that was copied from either Groq or ChatGPT into the header section.

Spoiler

6. Next, we need to enter our payload under the Body tab. The request payload format may vary significantly depending on the API provider; the example below is appropriate for Groq. We are using the {{current_message.content}} variable to connect the user prompt with the API payload, allowing the user’s query to be parsed and sent to the API for further processing.

For more detailed and binding methods, please refer to the official Conversational AI documentation.

 https://help.sap.com/docs/conversational-ai/concepts-of-sap-conversational-ai/body-configuration

Spoiler

7. The structure of the response can be viewed under the Response tab. We can use our reference namespace to capture the API response and display it in the bot’s next message.

Spoiler

8. we are required to parse the API's response into a message for our bot. To achieve this, select "Text Message" as the message type and enter the following code:{{api_service_response.resp.body.choices[0].message.content}}

Spoiler
9. Before sending another Yes or No prompt, allow the user to read the response for a few seconds. Choose "Button" as the message type and configure the buttons as shown in the snapshot. Each button will trigger specific actions:
  • If the user selects "No," the interaction will be routed to a different skill called "Customer Satisfaction Prompt," where we collect responses to assess if a fallback channel is needed to ensure user satisfaction.
  • If the user selects "Yes," choose "Postback" from the options, and the process will restart, allowing the user to ask another question.

This approach ensures a smooth feedback loop for continued interaction.

Spoiler
Spoiler

⚙️👨‍🔧Configuring Actions in the 'Customer Satisfaction Prompt' Skill:

To access the "Customer Satisfaction Prompt" skill, navigate to the Build tab. Here, you will set up a Yes or No response from the user to determine the appropriate next steps.

1. The operations in this skill are automatically populated by default. It will collect user responses, store them in memory, and then forward the call to the "Customer Satisfaction Reply" skill, where the fallback channel will be utilized.

Spoiler
2. The bot will conclude the chat with a welcome message and reset the conversation memory when the user replies "Yes" to the "Did I answer your question?" query.
Spoiler

3. If the user selects "No" to the question in the previous skill, the fallback channel will be triggered.

Spoiler

4. Since we have already set up the Intercom integration, select the appropriate Intercom channel name from the dropdown menu.

Spoiler
Spoiler

The bot is now ready for testing. If you wish, you can test it using the standalone WebKit URL and adjust the responses as needed. Once testing is complete, we can proceed with integrating the bot into our SAP Analytics Cloud dashboard.

🛠Equipping our 🤖 Bot into our SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboard :

1. I have integrated my bot into the dashboard using both the Web Widget and Popup Widget, accessible through a button. The Web Widget loads the bot, and the pop-up will open as needed. The pop-up can also be customized to align with the dashboard's theme.

2. A help button has been added to the upper-right corner of every page on the dashboard, including the landing page. This button opens a pop-up window containing the Web Widget with the bot.

Spoiler

3. The standalone WebKit URL can be used to set up the bot in the website widget.

Spoiler

That's all; we can add our bot to any part of the dashboard and call it using a button.

📌Testing the Bot in the SAP Analytics Cloud Environment :

1. The initial prompt starts with a welcome message, followed by a straightforward query. I selected a simple query that is already present in our bot’s knowledge base. As a result, the bot delivers a direct response from the knowledge base.

Spoiler

2. The bot will recognize when a query falls outside its knowledge base and, as per the design, will trigger a GPT callback to retrieve responses from LLM models. For the next query, which is beyond the bot's knowledge base, the bot will utilize Generative AI. I will ask, "How do I use the data analyzer in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)?" to prompt this behavior.

Spoiler

3. After providing ChatGPT's answer, the bot will ask a series of Yes/No questions to determine if the user is satisfied with the response. If the user indicates dissatisfaction, the bot will trigger a backup path for further assistance.
Spoiler

4. If the user selects "YES," a postback call is triggered, and the conversation will return to the FAQ skill, where the user can begin asking questions about the dashboard from the start.

5. If the user selects "NO," as per our configuration, the bot will route to the User Satisfaction Reply skill. Another Yes/No question "Did this answer your question?" will be asked.

6. If the user answers "YES," the bot will reset the memory and close the conversation. If the user answers "NO", the bot will redirect to the live chat powered by Intercom.

Spoiler
7. The bot’s live message is displayed on the Intercom site along with the user's chat history, providing the support representative with the context needed to address the support request accurately. From this point, the support representative can offer further assistance as required.
 
Below are a few exchanges from the perspectives of the support executive via the Intercom console and the bot in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC).
Spoiler




🔚Conclusion : 

The integration of the bot into SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), utilizing SAP Conversational AI and GPT, delivers a highly efficient, intelligent support system. By combining a well-structured knowledge base with advanced AI for handling complex queries, the bot ensures users receive prompt and accurate responses. Furthermore, the fallback to live chat with Intercom provides a seamless handover to human support when necessary, creating a well-rounded, responsive experience that enhances both user satisfaction and operational efficiency within SAC.

📢Feel free to contact me or leave a comment🗨 if you have any questions❓ or feedback📝!

Reference:

  • SAP Conversational A.I - https://help.sap.com/docs/conversational-ai/concepts-of-sap-conversational-ai/create-your-chatbot.
  • Chat GPT API - https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/introduction.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud - https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD

entry.title: Moving from Version 1 to Version 2 of the Enterprise Project OData API
entry.content:
Are you still using version 1 of the Enterprise Project OData API (API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV) to create, read, update, and delete enterprise projects in SAP S/4HANA Cloud? If so, this blog will help you to move smoothly to version 2 (API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV_0002).

Why You Should Move to the New Version

Version 1 of the Enterprise Project OData API was deprecated in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2102 and decommissioned in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2202. To prevent technical maintenance efforts for an outdated product version, SAP now plans to delete this API version soon. Ensure the stability of your processes and allow us to focus on new features for your benefit by moving to version 2 of theEnterprise Project OData API now.

Version 2 of the Enterprise Project OData API was introduced in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2102. In addition to enterprise projects (investment projects, overhead projects, statistical projects, and projects with revenue), this new API version also supports professional services projects (customer projects and internal projects) as well as project responsibilities (entitlements), and it has been enhanced continually with further features, such as work items, deliverables and additional properties. Take advantage of these new features by moving to version 2 of theEnterprise Project OData API now.

What You Need to Do

Version 2 of the Enterprise Project OData API is available on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub under Enterprise Project. The following features have been changed and require you to adjust your API requests when moving from version 1 to version 2 of the API: 

  • The API version you address in your HTTP requests
  • The processing of project manager data
  • The way you determine the position of project elements within the work breakdown structure
  • The operations for changing the processing status of the project definition and enterprise project elements

How to Adjust the API Version in Your Requests

To call version 2 of the Enterprise Project API, replace v=0001 withv=0002 in your requests.

Example – API Version 1:

POST <host>/sap/opu/odata/SAP/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0001/A_EnterpriseProject

Example – API Version 2:

POST  <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/A_EnterpriseProject

How to Adjust the Processing of Project Manager Data

In version 2 of the Enterprise Project API, the project manager data is no longer part of the header entity A_EnterpriseProject. Instead, you assign a project manager to a project using the new entities for project team members and their entitlements. Proceed as follows to adjust your requests:

  • When creating an enterprise project (HTTP method POST of the A_EnterpriseProject entity):
    1. Remove property ProjectManagerUUID from the header data in your request.

      Example:

      POST <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/A_EnterpriseProject

      {
      "Project": "TestProject",
      "ProjectDescription": "TestProjectDescription",
      "ProjectStartDate": "2023-01-07T00:00:00",
      "ProjectEndDate": "2023-12-07T00:00:00",
      "ResponsibleCostCenter": "17101501",
      "ProfitCenter": "YB600",
      "ProjectManagerUUID":"0894EF45-7D01-1EDA-B2E1-4D0D02BD4547",
      "ProjectProfileCode": "YP02",

    2. Assign the project manager as a team member to your project using entity A_EnterpriseProjectTeamMember; Assign the team member to the project role with the role type YP_RL_001 (Project Manager) using entity A_EntTeamMemberEntitlement. The project role is created automatically during project creation.

      Example:

      {
          "Project": "CXL230301Y1",
          "ProjectDescription": "CXL230301Y1",
          "ProjectStartDate": "2023-01-07T00:00:00",
          "ProjectEndDate": "2023-12-07T00:00:00",
          "ResponsibleCostCenter": "17101501",
          "ProfitCenter": "YB600",
          "ProjectProfileCode": "YP02",
          "to_EntProjTeamMember": [
             {
              "BusinessPartnerUUID": "42010AEF-4E36-1EDC-ADCB-3AC6A0784013",
              "to_EntProjEntitlement": [
                 {
                    "ProjectRoleType": "YP_RL_0001"
                 }
               ]
             }
          ]

      }

      For more information, see Create Project Definition, Create Team Member, Create Entitlement.

  • When reading the project manager data of an enterprise project (in API version 1, HTTP method GET of the A_EnterpriseProject entity):

Instead of retrieving the data of the header entity, use the HTTP method GET of the A_EntTeamMemberEntitlement and A_EnterpriseProjectTeamMember entities to read the entitlement and team member data of the project manager (project role type YP_RL_001).

Example:

GET <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/A_EnterpriseProjectRole?$filter=(ProjectRoleType eq 'YP_RL_0001' and ProjectUUID eq guid'e9d28130-f33a-1eef-96d4-c15bd5dbdba4')&$expand=to_EnterpriseProject/to_EntProjTeamMember

GET <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/A_EnterpriseProjectTeam Member(guid'0894EF45-7D01-1EDA-B2E1-4D0D02BD4547')/to_EntProjEntitlement

  • When updating the project manager data of an existing enterprise project (in API version 1, method PATCH of the A_EnterpriseProject entity):

    1. Remove property ProjectManagerUUID from the update request for header entity A_EnterpriseProject. If it is the only property to be updated, delete the request.

    2. If there is no project manager assigned to the project yet, create the team member and entitlement data for the project manager using the HTTP method POST of the A_EnterpriseProjectTeamMember entity.

      Example:

       

    3. If there is already a project manager assigned to the project:
      1. Delete the existing entitlement using the HTTP method DELETE of the A_EntTeamMemberEntitlement entity.

        Example:

        DELETE <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/A_EntTeamMemberEntitlement(guid'9418820a-c0a9-1ed8-9a92-4bed96f211dd')
      2. Create the team member and entitlement data for the new project manager using the HTTP method POST of the A_EnterpriseProjectTeamMember entity (see the example in step 2).

For more information, see Update Team Member Assigned to Entitlement

How to Adjust the Way you Determine the Position of Project Elements

In version 2 of the Enterprise Project API, property SortingNumber of the A_EnterpriseProjectElement entity has been replaced by property ProjectElementOrdinalNumber. Like the sorting number in API version 1, the ordinal number determines the sequence of project elements that have the same parent object in the work breakdown structure of an enterprise project. However, in API version 2, properties ProjectElementOrdinalNumber and ParentObjectUUID can no longer be changed using the update operation for the A_EnterpriseProjectElement entity. Instead, you need to use a dedicated function import for changing the position of project elements. Proceed as follows to adjust your requests:

  • When creating an enterprise project with project elements or creating project elements for an existing project (HTTP method POST of the A_EnterpriseProject or A_EnterpriseProjectElement entity):

Remove property SortingNumber in the project element data of your request. New project elements are added after existing project elements with the same parent object.

Example:

POST <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV ;v=0002/A_EnterpriseProjectElement

{

"ProjectUUID" : "0894ef45-77a9-1ee8-b8b2-f5dc22f3f345",

"ProjectElement" : "EXAMPLE_EPPM_PROJECT_ELEMENT",

"ProjectElementDescription" : "Example Enterprise Project Element”,

"PlannedStartDate" : "2019-01-01T00:00:00",

"PlannedEndDate" : "2019-12-31T00:00:00

}

ProjectElementOrdinalNumber is read-only and calculated. It’s helpful to identify the position of project element.

For more information, see Project Element and Create Project Element.

  • When reading the project element data of an enterprise project (for example, HTTP method GET of the A_EnterpriseProjectElement entity):

Instead of property SortingNumber, use property ProjectElementOrdinalNumber in combination with property ParentObjectUUID to determine the position of a project element.

  • When updating the position of an enterprise project (in API version 1, method PATCH of the A_ EnterpriseProjectElement entity):
    1. Remove properties ProjectElementOrdinalNumber and ParentObjectUUID from the update request for the A_EnterpriseProject entity. If these are the only properties to be updated, delete the request.
    2. Use the http method POST to call the ChangeEntProjElmntPosition function import and specify the new position of the project element using the parameter ProjectElementUUID, ParentObjectUUID, and LeftSiblingUUID.
    3. Parameter LeftSiblingUUID indicates the project element preceding the shifted project element within the sequence of project elements that have the same parent object.

Example:

POST <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/ChangeEntProjElmntPosition?ProjectElementUUID=guid'0894EF45-7741-1EDA-AEBA-0E072B97B70F'&ParentObjectUUID=guid'0894EF45-7741-1EDA-AEBA-0E072B97770F'&LeftSiblingUUID=guid'0894EF45-7741-1EDA-AEBA-0E072B99D70F'

In this example, project element 0894EF45-7741-1EDA-AEBA-0E072B97B70F is shifted to the structure level below project element 0894EF45-7741-1EDA-AEBA-0E072B97770F. In the sequence of project elements at this structure level, it is inserted as the direct successor of project element0894EF45-7741-1EDA-AEBA-0E072B99D70F.

For more information, see Change Position of Project Element.

How to Adjust the Operations for Changing the Processing Status

In version 2 of the Enterprise Project API, the actions for changing the processing status of the project definition and project elements have been replaced by two new function imports, which require the same parameters as the actions that they replace. Proceed as follows to adjust your requests:

  • When updating the processing status of the project definition (in API version 1, method POST for the SetProcessingStatus action):

In your request, replace SetProcessingStatus with ChangeEntProjProcgStatus.

Example:

POST <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/ChangeEntProjProcgStatus?ProjectUUID=guid'9418820a-c0a9-1ed8-9a92-4bed96f211dd'&ProcessingStatus='10'

  • When updating the processing status of a project element (in API version 1, method POST for the SetProjElemProcessingStatus action):

In your request, replace SetProcessingStatus with ChangeEntProjProcgStatus.

Example:

POST <host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_ENTERPRISE_PROJECT_SRV;v=0002/ChangeEntProjElmntProcgStatus?ProjectElementUUID=guid'1c98ec18-1855-1ed9-87db-9bca6b653165'&ProcessingStatus='10'

For more information, see Change Processing Status of Project Definition and Change Processing Status of Project Element.

Communication Arrangement

There is no need to change communication arrangement.

And you could see that the version 1 has been deprecated since 2102.

Summary

In this blog, we have summarized the changes that require your attention when moving from version 1 to version 2 of the Enterprise Project OData API. By providing step-by-step procedures and examples, we hope to guide you through this transition as seamlessly as possible. Please feel free to ask any further question that you have related to this.

entry.title: Re: Transitioning Flat File Storage from Shared Disk to M...
entry.content:
Hi Norman

 

BW can read data from two places:

  • Local workstation
  • Application server

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_740/ccc9cdbdc6cd4eceaf1e5485b1bf8f4b/4a181c192886044fe10000000a421937-155.html?locale=en-US

So in order to read a file from a Sharepoint folder, that must first be mounted to the file system of either your local workstation or the file system of the ABAP application server. Possible example resources for mapping:

  • Windows:
    • https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/614462/linking-sharepoint-site-to-a-network-drive
    • Or just use the OneDrive integration
  • Linux: https://www.reddit.com/r/sysadmin/comments/cnqjan/how_to_mount_sharepoint_online_in_linux/

br

Roland

entry.title: Re: Join tables vbak and konv
entry.content:
I can see that you are using strict OpenSQL and that means your system version is quite modern, that KONV is transparent table already. Please clarify what exact problem do you have with join. what exact problem with snippet like that in your system? Could you try it?

 

  SELECT vkorg, vbeln, erdat, vtweg, kalsm
   FROM vbak as vbak
      join konv as konv
      on vbak~knumv eq konv~knumv
    WHERE vbak~erdat IN @s_date AND
          vbak~vkorg IN @s_vkorg AND
          vbak~vtweg IN @s_vtweg AND
          vbak~vbeln IN @s_vbeln
      and konv~kdatu IN @s_kdatu AND
          konv~kposn IN @s_kposn AND
          konv~stunr IN @s_stunr AND
          konv~kschl IN @s_kschl AND
          konv~kherk IN @s_kherk AND
          konv~kinak IN @s_kinak
   INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @IT_vbak.

entry.title: 2412 Release Highlights of SAP Signavio Process Insights
entry.content:
SAP Signavio Process Insights December release 2412 brings again a couple of great enhancement.


  • Frequent blockers on the Process Flows screen display now the number of associated correction recommendations
  • Filters in Value Analysis
  • Percentage Distribution Chart in Trend Analysis for Frequent Blockers
  • New System Configuration Tab
  • 11 new standard performance indicators for project management and 11 new correction recommendations for material requirements planning. 
  • Guided analysis is now available for two additional standard performance indicators
  • New correction recommendation for one standard performance indicator in finance
  • 7 new innovation recommendations have been added for CRM and Customer Experience (3) and SAP Fiori Apps (4)

You can see this in action together with Process Intelligence and Plug and Gain:

Here you can learn more:

  1. See SAP Signavio Process Insights, Process Intelligence and Plug & Gain in action
  2. Your first steps to get started with SAP Signavio Process Insights
  3. Who do I need to involve and which challenges do I want to solve?
  4. How to start your analysis?
  5. “Cash is King” - but how to become royal? - SAP Community
  6. Why are there two business process analysis soluti... - SAP Community
  7. Plug & Gain:

Plug & Gain Video Series and Plug & Gain OpenSAP
How to use the Plug & Gain Connector
Optimize financial closing process with Plug & Gain

  1. SAP Analytics Cloud Content for Process Insights
  2. SAP Learning:
    SAP Signavio Process Analysis
    SAP Signavio Process Management & Design
    SAP Signavio Solution Configuration
  3. Partner Edge (Login required): Webinar Series for Process Insights
  4. SAP Signavio Process Insights "Whats New?"
  5. SAP Signavio Process Insights Help

entry.title: Re: Join tables vbak and konv
entry.content:
I guess you missed aliases somewhere for join. Try the code below. I have checked in 750 - it is working. it should work. Select-Options as in your sample. Everything should be fine 🙂 

TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_tab,
         vkorg TYPE  vbak-vkorg,
         vbeln TYPE  vbak-vbeln,
         erdat TYPE  vbak-erdat,
         vtweg TYPE  vbak-vtweg,
         kalsm TYPE  vbak-kalsm,
         knumv TYPE  vbak-knumv,
         kposn TYPE konv-kposn,
         stunr TYPE konv-stunr,
         kschl TYPE konv-kschl,
         kdatu TYPE konv-kdatu,
         kherk TYPE konv-kherk,
         kinak TYPE konv-kinak,
       END OF ty_tab.

data  it_tab   TYPE TABLE OF ty_tab.

  SELECT
      vbak~vkorg, vbak~vbeln, vbak~erdat, vbak~vtweg, vbak~kalsm,
    vbak~knumv,
      konv~kposn, konv~stunr,  konv~kschl, konv~kdatu,
        konv~kherk, konv~kinak
   FROM vbak as vbak
      join konv as konv
      on vbak~knumv eq konv~knumv
    WHERE vbak~erdat IN @s_date AND
          vbak~vkorg IN @s_vkorg AND
          vbak~vtweg IN @s_vtweg AND
          vbak~vbeln IN @s_vbeln
      and konv~kdatu IN @s_kdatu AND
          konv~kposn IN @s_kposn AND
          konv~stunr IN @s_stunr AND
          konv~kschl IN @s_kschl AND
          konv~kherk IN @s_kherk AND
          konv~kinak IN @s_kinak
   INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @IT_tab.

entry.title: How to Monitor IBM Db2 LUW Database Performance – Part 1: MONREPORT Module
entry.content:
This is part 1 of 2 blog posts on how you can best monitor your Db2 for Linux, UNIX, and Windows (LUW) database. As a system administrator, you probably ask yourself the following questions:

  • How can I see if my Db2 database performs well?
  • What can I do to check and analyze the performance of my database?
  • In its documentation, Db2 provides a long list of monitor elements. What are the different ways how I can query them?
  • Which monitor elements should I choose and how can I interpret their values?

In this blog post, I’ll have a closer look on the Db2 monitor elements and show you how you can categorize them. I’ll show you some of the routines included in the MONREPORT module as an example of how to check the Db2 monitor elements from the command line. The MONREPORT module provides an easy and very structured approach to querying the monitor elements. But first you need to understand the Db2 monitor elements.

 

Db2 Monitor Elements

In the IBM Db2 documentation, you can find  a long list of monitor elements in alphabetical order here: Monitor element reference - IBM Documentation. For each monitor element you get a list of monitoring table functions that are using it. To check and analyze the performance of your Db2 database, you use the time-spent monitor elements, which show you on which activities Db2 spends its time. But often you can find time-spent monitor elements with similar names, for example, TOTAL_COMMIT_TIME and TOTAL_COMMIT_PROC_TIME. This can be confusing. Sorting the time-spent monitor elements into different categories can help you to get a better understanding.

System and Activity Dimension

Db2 categorizes the time-spent monitor elements into two different dimensions: The first dimension is System Level, the second one Activity Level. If you want to check how your database performs as a whole, you need monitor elements on system level. If you are interested in the performance of a certain database activity like an SQL query, you must look for monitor elements on activity level.

Processing, Wait, and Elapse Time

For both dimensions – system level and activity level – two different perspectives exist which you can use to find out where Db2 exactly spends its time. The first perspective compares processing times with wait times. Processing time could be the time Db2 needs to perform a backup, to compile queries, or to execute queries. Wait times can be spent in waiting for locks to be released or for data to be read from disk into the buffer pool. The second perspective compares elapse times with processing times. Processing times are a subset of the elapse times. This means that the elapse time includes the processing time and various wait times. The wait times included in the elapse time are not necessarily tracked by Db2 in monitor elements. Therefore, it makes no sense to compare elapse and wait times.

Distinguishing Between Monitor Elements with Similar Names

With this knowledge, you can now find out the difference between the monitor elements with similar names. For example, the difference between TOTAL_COMMIT_TIME and TOTAL_COMMIT_PROC_TIME:
TOTAL_COMMIT_TIME is the elapse time the database spent for committing transactions. Because it is the elapse time, TOTAL_COMMIT_TIME can include wait times like waiting for I/O operations or waiting for log file operations to complete. TOTAL_COMMIT_PROC_TIME reflects only the processing time Db2 needed to execute commit statements and includes no wait times. In this blog post, I’ll focus on the first perspective and therefore will use and compare only processing and wait times.

Options to Monitor Db2 from the Command Line

In the past, a common way to monitor a Db2 database from the command line were snapshots which you can call with the GET SNAPSHOT command. Snapshots provide a human-readable and well-arranged output. For monitoring, an important option of the snapshots was that you could reset the data collection. That means, after a reset you get only the values collected since the reset – and not since the database was started. This is important and very helpful to find peaks in resource usage and issues with your database workload. But the snapshots were no longer enhanced since Db2 version 9.7 and new monitor elements were not included into them. Therefore, you should look for more current ways to monitor your database.

MON_GET Table Function

A newer and better option to monitor Db2 databases are the MON_GET table functions. You can find all MON_GET table functions in the IBM Db2 documentation, for example under Monitor procedures and functions - IBM Documentation. The MON_GET table functions are still being enhanced from time to time and include all monitor elements, especially new monitor elements for new Db2 features. You can easily query the MON_GET table functions via SQL. But for the MON_GET table functions, no reset option exists. This means that the monitor elements return the time spent for a certain Db2 component since the database was started or since the activity was started. You will have to calculate the deltas between your regular data collections to be able to identify, for example, peaks in resource usage. Another difference to the snapshots is the output: You don’t get a nice, human-readable list of the different monitor elements. So, querying the MON_GET table functions with SQL is flexible and easy to consume from an application. But for ad-hoc monitoring, you need a simpler command which you can easily call from the command line and which provides a general overview. An option that combines the usage of the MON_GET table functions with a nice human-readable output are the routines from the MONREPORT module. Additionally, the MONREPORT routines do the delta calculation for you.

MONREPORT Module

Now let’s have a closer look on three routines of the MONREPORT module. You can find more information about the MONREPORT module under Reports generated using the MONREPORT module - IBM Documentation.

The first one is MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY. Remember the dimensions and perspectives for the time-spent monitor elements described before. MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY gives you an overview on system level. For the activity level, I’ll use two other MONREPORT routines: MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL and MONREPORT.CONNECTION.

All three MONREPORT routines compare processing times with wait times. Most of the points I’ll discuss apply to all Db2 databases, regardless of the application using it. But there are one or two points where we have a special situation for SAP environments.

 

MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY

You call MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY with the following command:
db2 "CALL MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY(<time interval>)"

As input value, MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY needs a time interval of type INTEGER. For example, if you specify “30” as time interval, MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY collects monitoring data for the first time when it is started and will do a second data collection 30 seconds later. MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY calculates the delta values between the two data collections. The default time interval is 10 seconds.

Output Report

All routines from module MONREPORT provide you with an output report. The output report of all MONREPORT routines starts with a header, which provides the name of the database and the time stamp when the report was generated. For MONREPORT routines that are using a time interval like MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY, you will also find the used collection interval in the header.

After this header, the further output of MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY consists of three parts. The first part provides information about the overall system performance; the information in the second part helps you drill down to application performance, and the third part provides information on member level.

System Performance

Let’s now have a closer look on the first part where you can find the monitor elements TOTAL_APP_COMMITS, ACT_COMPLETED_TOTAL, APP_RQSTS_COMPLETED_TOTAL, the CPU time that was spent by Db2, and information about row processing.

You can use monitor the TOTAL_APP_COMMITS element to identify how many commits were issued by your application. You can use the value of TOTAL_APP_COMMITS as a base level measurement of system activity. The value provided in column Total shows you the amount of commits for the complete collection time interval. MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY also calculates the number of commits per second. If you execute MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY regularly, you can compare those values to find out during which times your database is busy. 

Monitor element ACT_COMPLETED_TOTAL shows you the number of successfully completed activities on your database, and APP_RQSTS_COMPLETED_TOTAL the number of submitted requests.

Requests and Activities

Db2 distinguishes between requests and activities. A request is any kind of directive to a database agent to perform work that needs database resources like CPU and memory.  Activities are a subset of requests and mainly refer to the work done to execute SQL statements. You can find more information about requests and activities in the IBM Db2 documentation. A good starting point are, for example, the following pages:
Request monitor elements - IBM Documentation
Activity monitor elements - IBM Documentation.

TOTAL_CPU_TIME provides you the total amount of CPU time used within the database system. MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY also calculates the TOTAL_CPU_TIME per request.

ROWS_READ and ROWS_RETURNED

The comparison of ROWS_READ with ROWS_RETURNED can help you to find out how efficient your queries work. A low value for ROWS_READ/ROWS_RETURNED means that you have sufficient indexes that help the SQL statements to quickly find the needed data. A high value here can indicate insufficient or even missing indexes. Additionally, with ROWS_MODIFIED, you get the number of table rows changed via SQL statements like INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. This information gives you a first idea about the workload on your database.

Wait Times

In the next section of the MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY output, you get further information about wait times.

The first output shows you how much time Db2 spent for waiting in general. MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY compares the total processing time with the total wait time separately again for requests and activities. MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY calculates the percentage of the wait time compared to the total time. If you find a high percentage of wait time here, you should analyze this further in the detailed breakdown section for the wait time, which is the next part of the output report.

Watch out for CLIENT_IDLE_WAIT_TIME

But first some comments on CLIENT_IDLE_WAIT_TIME: In an SAP environment, be careful with the interpretation of CLIENT_IDLE_WAIT_TIME. Since the SAP application processes are all the time connected to the database, a high CLIENT_IDLE_WAIT_TIME does not necessarily mean that there is a performance issue on the client side. If the performance looks good from database side, you can use SAP transactions like ST03 to further analyze the application performance.

Breakdown of Wait Times

Let’s continue with the output of MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY. As already mentioned above, the next part of the output report provides you with further details about the wait times and splits the TOTAL_WAIT_TIME into more detailed monitor elements.

In this detailed output, you can see what percentage of the wait time is used for I/O wait time, like, for example, buffer pool read time (POOL_READ_TIME), or for waiting on locks (LOCK_WAIT_TIME) or the network. This overview does not reflect all potential wait times, but it gives you a first idea in which direction you should further check in the case of high wait times.

Processing Times

High wait times can point to a general performance issue on your Db2 database. Maybe the buffer pool is too small for your workload? Then you would have a high buffer pool read time. But what about high processing times? Can they also be an issue? To answer these questions, use section Detailed breakdown of processing time in the output of the MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY. In this section, the total database processing time is divided into more detailed categories such as section execution time, compile time, or the time spent for executing utilities like a table reorganization or data load.

If the wait times in section Detailed breakdown of TOTAL_WAIT_TIME show that there might be an issue with the buffer pool, you can use the information in section Buffer pool to analyze this further.

Buffer Pool Hit Ratios

The buffer pool hit ratios – also referred to as buffer pool quality – show you what percentage of the data needed for SQL query processing was found in the buffer pool. If you access data, for example, from a table, Db2 reads the data from disk into memory. The memory part where the data is stored is called buffer pool. Db2 tries to avoid too many disk accesses because they are expensive. If the needed data was found in the buffer pool, we call this logical data access. If Db2 must access the data on disk, we speak of a physical data read. A high buffer pool hit ratio means that most of the data was found in the buffer pool and only a few physical data accesses were needed.

Rule of thumb: The higher the buffer pool hit ratio, the better the system performance.

Even a buffer pool hit ratio of 98% can point to an issue with memory and especially the buffer pool size. A buffer pool hit ratio below 98% is definitely not good and should be checked further.

The hit ratio as well as the number of buffer pool reads are listed for different types of data, for example, DATA for table data, INDEX for indexes on tables, and TEMP for temporary data.

Direct I/O

Direct I/O means data access without using the buffer pool. This is the case, for example, for LOB objects. What might also be interesting: The number of log disk waits (LOG_DISK_WAITS_TOTAL) which means how often the database has to wait for writing a log entry for changing data with INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE.

Application Performance Breakdown

Going further through the output of MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY, you now get to the second part, which is Application performance drill down. It gives you detailed information about the different database connections.

Here, in the second part of the MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY output, you find the four monitor elements TOTAL_CPU_TIME, TOTAL_WAIT_TIME, TOTAL_APP_COMMITS, and ROWS_READ together with ROWS_MODIFIED for all database connections. The values for these four monitor elements are listed below for each connection to the database separately.

These monitor elements help you identify candidates for further analysis and for tuning. A high CPU usage can be a sign for a less than optimal access plan. High wait times should be narrowed further down to see if, for example, lock wait times or buffer pool read times are causing them. The number of commits (TOTAL_APP_COMMITS) can be used together with other monitor elements to calculate how often something happens for one transaction. For example, how many lock waits or deadlocks occur for each transaction. A high number of ROWS_READ and ROWS_MODIFIED can be a sign for a heavily used table and maybe also for an insufficient table access caused, for example, by a missing or suboptimal index. I’ll discuss MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL and MONREPORT.CONNECTION, which provide information about database activities, later in this blog post.

Member Level Information

In the third and last part of the MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY output, you get performance information about each database member. This output can be helpful for Db2 pureScale systems.

More Information

For more information about MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY, see the IBM Db2 documentation at DBSUMMARY procedure - Generate a summary report of system and application performance metrics - IBM Documentation.

 

MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL

The first routine you can use to get more information about the currently executed SQL statements is MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL. You can call it as follows:
db2 "CALL MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL"
As opposed to MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY, you cannot set a time interval here. In the header at the beginning of the output report, only the database name and the timestamp of the data collection are displayed.

Output Report

The output report of MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL consists of three parts:

In the first part Summaries by 'top' metrics, you find the top 10 activities for three different monitor elements. The first one is again the CPU usage (TOTAL_CPU_TIME), the second one is the number of ROWS_READ, and the third one is DIRECT_READS and DIRECT_WRITES.

I already mentioned the CPU usage and that a high number of ROWS_READ can be a sign for a heavily used table. But what about the direct reads and writes? Direct reads and writes do not use the buffer pool which applies, for example, for queries accessing LOB data.

Ranking of Activities

In the second part of MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL, you find a ranking of all activities over the three monitor elements.

Here in this example, you can see that application handle 20754 has the highest ranking for ROWS_READ, but that it is only in third place for CPU time. The next application handle in the list (11000) has the highest ranking for CPU usage but only fourth place for ROWS_READ. They all have the same amount of direct reads and writes (eighth place). This ranking helps you identify further expensive queries that could be candidates for tuning.

Statement Text

In the third part of MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL, you can finally find the complete statement text for all activities listed above.

More Information

For more information about MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL, see the IBM Db2 documentation CURRENTSQL procedure - Generate a report that summarizes activities - IBM Documentation.

If you are familiar with the application snapshots, you’ll probably miss information about the client process that executed the statements. In an application snapshot, you can get the client PID, the name of the client user, the client application name, and much more information. This information can be helpful to find out which client program exactly is causing the performance issues on the database.

You can find this information also in the MONREPORT module, so let’s have a closer look at MONREPORT.CONNECTION.

 

MONREPORT.CONNECTION

You can call MONREPORT.CONNECTION as follows:
db2 "call MONREPORT.CONNECTION(<time interval>)"

As you can already see from the command, MONREPORT.CONNECTION also works with a time interval like MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY. You find information about the time interval in the header of the output from MONREPORT.CONNECTION together with the database name and the collection timestamp.

Output Report

The output file generated by MONREPORT.CONNECTION has only two parts. The first part shows a summary of all connections to the database. Again, you get here the monitor elements TOTAL_CPU_TIME, TOTAL_ACT_TIME, ACT_COMPLETED, and TOTAL_WAIT_TIME as well as CLIENT_IDLE_WAIT_TIME, which I already discussed above for MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY.

Remember, in an SAP environment, you have to be careful with the monitor element CLIENT_IDLE_WAIT_TIME: Since for an SAP system, the application is always connected to the database, the output of CLIENT_IDLE_WAIT_TIME can be misleading and high values for this monitor element do not necessarily point to an issue with the application. If you think there might be a performance issue on SAP application side, you can analyze this further using SAP tools like transaction ST03.

Database Connections

It is the second part of the MONREPORT.CONNECTION output that provides you with further information about the database connections you are looking for.

In the second part Details for each connection, you find for each connection the application name and client attributes such as the client user or application name. After that, you get the same monitor elements for each connection as in MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY for the complete database. Since I’ve already listed those monitor elements above on database level, I’m not doing it again here.

More Information

For more details about MONREPORT.CONNECTION see the IBM Db2 documentation under CONNECTION procedure - Generate a report on connection metrics - IBM Documentation.

 

Summary

I hope this blog post helped you to become more familiar with the Db2 monitor elements and that sorting them into categories helps you to interpret them. You can use the MONREPORT routines MONREPORT.DBSUMMARY, MONREPORT.CURRENTSQL, and MONREPORT.CONNECTION to monitor your Db2 database and to get a first overview on the system and activity performance. If you need more detailed information, you can use the MON_GET table functions of Db2 to query Db2 monitor elements specifically for your issue.

entry.title: Re: WT for flat-rate SV-contributions for Employer DE
entry.content:
Will that WT be transfered to FI as an expense or will it only be used as an "Information WT" ?

Does such an Information WT already exist in your system so that you could copy it? 

Will the WT in IT0015 be a "trigger" for the calculation (that will be based on an other WT) or will it hold the amount on which to calculate the SV- contribution?

Execute Payroll Simulation for one employee, with the "Display Log" box checked, and drill down into that log to find where the WT on which you want to base the SV- contribution exists with it's Amount.  That would be a good place to insert a PCR to calculate your Information WT (using a Custom SV-Contribution Constant in V_T511K that holds the value of 21.00).

Test and test, and for the payslip, don't configure your new WT in the Remuneration Statement if that's what you use, and if not, use table view V_512W_O to identify the differences between your new WT and one that does not appear on your payslip, in the section at the bottom of the screen (from V_512W_D if I remember correctly).

entry.title: Re: AGGREGATION ON CDS VIEW and Filtering data with where clause issue
entry.content:
Okay, I had the same kind of weird issues with just a simple SELECT LABST FROM CDS1 which gave a wrong result (without even joining to CDS2). ABAP 7.58.

Probably that using replaced objects like MARD (it has a replacement object named NSDM_E_MARD) may lead to many possible errors. Use the replacement object and I guess it will solve.

I don't know any way to check inconsistencies.

Replacement Objects - ABAP Keyword Documentation

  • When you define a replacement object for a database table for a classic database view, the system checks whether the specified CDS view fulfills the prerequisites. If a CDS view used as a replacement object is changed later on in such a way that the prerequisites are no longer fulfilled, a runtime error DBSQL_REDIRECT_INCONSISTENCY occurs when an Open SQL read is performed on the database table or on the database view.

  • You should be extremely careful when specifying a proxy object for a database table or a database view. Incorrect usage can cause inconsistencies.

LABST is no more stored in MARD, it can be accessed correctly via NSDM_E_MARD: 3450094 - CDC is not working if the calculated field is changed - SAP for Me.

When reading MARD, the replacement object should be automatically used, but it seems it's not... (maybe some bugs here)

entry.title: Unable to delete Quantity Conversion in Product Master Data
entry.content:
Error occurs while trying to update the quantity conversion in the product master data.
Error: 'Changing the conversion not possible; it is used by one or more open customer invoices, items to be invoiced, procurement price lists, purchase orders, purchase requests, sales orders, source documents, source documents for supplier invoices, supplier invoices and supplier credit memos'

Is it possible to change/delete the quantity conversion?

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Unable to delete Quantity Conversion in Product Maste...
entry.content:
The relevant material or service is already used in one or more documents. The reason for this error is that the business documents which are using this material would end up in inconsistency if the change is allowed. The veto check executed is not at quantity conversion level, but rather at material/service level. If that material/service is being used in a document, even if the quantity conversion used is one other than the one you intend to delete or change, the system will still block the action.

Since the quantity conversions are already used in business documents, system will not allow the changes at master data level. Changes to the quantity conversion directly in the backend are also not supported by SAP. As a workaround, SAP advises to create a new material with the same details as the existing one, maintaining the correct quantity conversions, then blocking the previous material/service for further use.

entry.title: Re: Delta processing inside SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Hello @Thibault_dockx,

in my demo case I only have 53k records with 2 medified records. And the runtime was 3 seconds. But in one of my customer we had 1 Billion records on SAP HANA Native On Premise and I used Flowgraph to do the same with HASH and MD5 function. And it worked well, since this HASH and MD5 functions create 32 string long and to compare 32 chars is much faster then to compare each fields in a row. The flowgraph was crashed if I compare each field with memory alllocation error.

Best regards

Wanda Soetomo

entry.title: Unlocking Business Process Potential with SAP Signavio Process Insights, Discovery Edition
entry.content:
Just want a crisp summary?
Read the abstract below in italics:

We examines two distinct approaches for business process analysis and improvement within the SAP ecosystem 

  • Business Process Improvement for SAP Solutions (SAP Signavio Process Insights, discovery edition) –For rapidly identifying and addressing high-level process inefficiencies and providing guiding improvements, based on Process Performance Indicators (PPIs).
  • Process Mining (SAP Signavio Process Intelligence or Process Mining by Celonis) – A data-driven, algorithmic approach to reconstruct and analyze real-world business processes using event logs.

The goal of this blog is to evaluate these approaches in terms of prerequisites, effort, cost, functionality, and applicability, determining their potential complementary.

Table of Comparison
The approaches are compared using specific criteria:

Criteria

SAP Business Process Improvement
(Signavio Process Insights)

Process Mining
(SAP Signavio Process Intelligence or Process Mining by Celonis)

Prerequisites

Requires SAP system access

Requires detailed event logs, ETL processes, and significant data preparation

Effort & Time

Lower initial setup, quick insights (minutes)

Higher setup complexity (months) and deeper insights

Cost

Free (discovery edition) or minimal licensing cost

High licensing costs

Functionality

High-level disagnosis,  predefined recommendations for recurring  issues

Deep dive into process deviations and complex scenarios, involving multiple solutions

Objectivity

Relies on predefined Process Performance Indicators, and user interpretation (or deeper analysis via process mining)

Algorithm-driven, objective insights from data

Use cases

Best to identify improvement opportunities across broad processes running on an SAP system

Ideal for analyzing critical or complex processes spanning on multiple solutions in depth

At the opposite to SAP Signavio Process Insights, SAP Signavio Process Intelligence is fully custumizable and editor/system agnostic.


Conclusion and Recommendations

Complementary Usage:

  • SAP Signavio Process Insights, discovery edition is recommended for rapid diagnosis and as a starting point for process improvement
  • Process mining is better suited for deeper investigations, especially in critical processes with substantial improvement potential.

Practical Implications:

  • SAP Signavio Process Insights identifies “where” to focus improvement efforts, while Process Mining answers “why” and “how” for detailed optimization.
  • Combining both approaches allows organizations to leverage the strengths of each. To assist with this,  you can leverage the Plug and Gain approach.

 -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Detailed blog:

In an era where process efficiency determines competitive edge, businesses are leaning heavily on digital solutions to analyze and optimize their workflows. The free-of-charge SAP Signavio Process Insights, discovery edition, is a solution crafted to analyze process performance within SAP ERP systems, enabling organizations to pinpoint inefficiencies and seize improvement opportunities. While process mining tools like Process Mining by Celonis and SAP Signavio Process Intelligence revolutionize business process analysis, SAP Signavio Process Insights offers a unique approach tailored for rapid insights and actionable outcomes, particularly within the SAP landscape.

Let’s dive into the value this solution offers, its unique differentiators, and how it complements—or diverges from—the broader field of process mining.

 

The Value of SAP Signavio Process Insights

1. Swift, Insight-Driven Analysis

SAP Signavio Process Insights enables organizations to quickly gain visibility into how their business processes are performing. Its built-in capability to analyze operational data within SAP ERP makes it accessible to users without requiring extensive setup or expertise. The discovery edition provides key metrics and identifies inefficiencies in areas such as order-to-cash or procure-to-pay processes, delivering a clear roadmap for improvement.

You can read more about SAP Signavio Process Insights, discovery edition on this page


2. Accelerating Transformation Initiatives

The solution serves as a foundational tool for businesses embarking on transformation journeys. By identifying bottlenecks, unnecessary manual tasks, and deviations from best practices, it sets the stage for deeper optimization projects. Organizations can address low-hanging fruits to realize quick wins, which is critical for maintaining momentum in transformation initiatives.

Finally, the solution provides contextually relevant suggestions for resolution (correction recommendations) and innovation recommendations.


3. Ease of Use and Cost Efficiency

Unlike many advanced tools that require substantial financial and resource investments, the discovery edition is free -for every SAP maintenance customers-. It allows businesses to gain process insights without the need for additional infrastructure investments, making it particularly valuable for organizations seeking to maximize ROI on existing systems.

 

Differentiating SAP Signavio Process Insights from Process Mining

While SAP Signavio Process Insights and process mining both aim to improve business process outcomes, they do so through fundamentally different methodologies and focus areas. Let’s explore these differences.

 

1. Breadth vs. Depth

Process mining tools, such as Celonis and SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, excel in granular, data-driven process exploration. They rely on event logs extracted from various systems, enabling a detailed reconstruction of process flows. This level of depth makes them ideal for diagnosing complex issues and optimizing critical business processes at a microscopic level.

SAP Signavio Process Insights, in contrast, is designed for speed and accessibility. Its focus is on providing a high-level analysis of process performance across SAP ERP systems, surfacing key improvement areas without delving into the intricate details of process variations or individual case analyses. This makes it a perfect starting point for businesses looking to identify where to focus their improvement efforts.

 

2. Implementation Complexity

SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, Celonis and other process mining tools often require significant effort to extract, transform, and load (ETL) data from disparate systems. These steps, combined with the need to create tailored data models, can make process mining a resource-intensive endeavor.

SAP Signavio Process Insights bypasses this complexity by working directly with existing SAP ERP configurations. It leverages pre-defined process content and KPIs, eliminating the need for complex data preparation while ensuring results are immediately actionable.

But what if you wish to get the best of both worlds (fast and deep insights) in an easy way?
For this, you can leverage the Plug and Gain approach.
We will briefly touch base on it below.

 

3. Use Cases: Quick Diagnosis vs. Comprehensive Analysis

SAP Signavio Process Insights is designed to identify systemic inefficiencies and improvement opportunities rapidly. For example, if an organization faces delays in procurement approvals, the solution highlights this issue and provides contextually relevant suggestions for resolution.

Process mining tools shine when there’s a need for comprehensive root-cause analysis or when investigating non-linear process flows. They uncover insights such as rework loops, compliance violations, and performance deviations across multiple systems, making them indispensable for highly complex and high-stakes processes.

 

4. Complementary Potential

Rather than viewing SAP Signavio Process Insights and process mining as competing solutions, businesses should consider how these tools can complement each other. SAP Signavio Process Insights is the perfect tool for identifying where process mining can provide the most value. For instance, businesses can use SAP Signavio Process Insights to identify critical bottlenecks and then employ SAP Signavio Process Intelligence to perform a deep dive into these areas, uncovering root causes and designing precise interventions.

This is where Plug and Gain comes into play.
In a nutshell, this approach uses scripts to convert data extracted by SAP Signavio Process Insights into event logs, that can be consumed by SAP Signavio Process Intelligence.

It can provides several complementary out-of-box content:

  • The Process Landscape: to understand the current process executions and performance across all E2E-processes. Identify areas with improvement potential, in particular transformation related
  • Advance Packages on an End-to-end Process: In-depth analysis of a dedicated process in questions related to transformation and continuous improvement. 8 end-to-end processes are currently available.

This approach is free, but requires access to both solutions (SAP Signavio Process Insights and SAP Signavio Process Intelligence).
You can learn more about Plug and gain here and you can get started here.

 

Why Choose SAP Signavio Process Insights, Discovery Edition?

For businesses running SAP ERP, the discovery edition of SAP Signavio Process Insights is a game-changer. Its pre-integrated setup ensures a seamless experience, and its focus on rapid insights aligns with today’s fast-paced business demands. Here’s why you should consider adopting this tool:

  • Ease of Adoption: No need for custom configurations or integrations
  • Immediate Value: Gain actionable insights into process performance without additional cost
  • Bridge to Deeper Analysis: Use it as a gateway to identify areas that warrant further investigation with advanced tools like SAP Signavio Process Intelligence levaring the Plug and Gain approach.


Closing Thoughts

SAP Signavio Process Insights, discovery edition, underscores SAP’s commitment to making process improvement accessible to all. It empowers organizations to take their first steps toward process excellence with minimal investment and effort. While tools like Celonis Process Mining and SAP Signavio Process Intelligence remain unparalleled for in-depth process exploration, SAP Signavio Process Insights offers an unbeatable combination of simplicity, speed, and scalability.

As businesses strive for greater efficiency, the ability to rapidly uncover improvement areas while leveraging existing systems is invaluable. By integrating SAP Signavio Process Insights into their process optimization toolkit, organizations can ensure they are equipped to thrive in today’s dynamic business landscape.

Let the journey to process excellence begin!

init: function() {

            }

 

 Reference links:

  • Developing Reuse Components 

  • sap.suite.ui.generic.template.extensionAPI.ReuseComponentSupport.mixInto

entry.title: Re: SAP crystal reports for visual studio 2019
entry.content:
Add this page to your Favourites:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/crystalreports/3354091173.html

On that page is a link to download as well as the Licensing info:

Crystal Reports for Visual Studio Licensing

Quick answer is you only need to purchase a Copy of Crystal Reports Designer per user developing your app.

https://www.sap.com/products/technology-platform/crystal-reports.html

So yes it is free as long as your app is not hosted on a Server or Hosting Site. See page 5 on the Licensing doc.

See this blog I wrote for upgrading, it has other info that helps also:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/upgrading-a-visual-studio-2008-gt-2022-net-project-with-the-latest-cr-net/ba-p/13469215

Note there are 2 installers (EXE), one for VS 2010->2019 (32bit) and one for VS 2022 (64bit) use the 32 bit for VS 2019. You have the correct one.

FYI, 32 bit is going away, SAP highly recommends you move to 64 bit platform:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/crystalreports/3354088411.html

(**) 32bit CR .NET Runtime will be discontinued after Dec 2025. Move to 64bit CR .NET Runtime ASAP.

You can build your distribution installer using one of the other MSI's, Zip (MSI) or Merge Module, EXE installs look for VS install and for DEV PC's only. And no you cannot manually distribute CR runtime, one of the deployment packages only, to many Registry keys and COM Dll's that need to be registered and no documentation on what they are.

I suggest moving to VS 2022 or when you build your app set it to x64 and not AnyCPU. CR runtime is for your app platform and not the OS.

On the link above there is more info for you also:

Related Documents

Crystal Reports for Visual Studio Licensing

Getting started and moving ahead with Crystal Reports .NET applications

See this Blog for instructions upgrading your existing project to the latest SP

See this blog on how to use CR 2020 Export to XLXS page formats:

How to use the new CR 2020 export to Microsoft Excel(XLXS) in Visual Studio .NET code (new)

how-to-parameters-in-crystal-reports-for-visual-studio-net

Printing Crystal Reports in .NET

SAP Crystal Reports for Visual Studio Installation Guide ( SP 21 and above)

Hope that helps

Don

entry.title: Re: Delta processing inside SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Hello @GabTan ,

the problem is, that we can not use Delta Capture activated local Table as target in DataFlow to extract data directly from source system via remote table. To use Delta Capture activated local Table as target you have to use Transformation Flow, but if you have remote table as data source you can not use Transformation Flow to extract data from source system. 🙄

That is the reason why I have written this blog and hope that SAP will provide us the solution for this issue. The other option would be, to use Delta capable ABAP CDS View in combination with Replication Flow to get the data per Delta into SAP Datasphere and after that to process the delta records in SAp Datasphere using Transformation Flow.

Best regards

Wanda Soetomo

entry.title: Re: Join tables vbak and konv
entry.content:
In that case you should replace move-corresponding to loop. The variant could be as below

  SELECT vkorg, vbeln, erdat, vtweg, kalsm, knumv
    FROM vbak
    INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @IT_vbak
      WHERE erdat IN @s_date AND
            vkorg IN @s_vkorg AND
            vtweg IN @s_vtweg AND
            vbeln IN @s_vbeln
      .

  IF it_vbak IS NOT INITIAL. " for FAE is obligatory
    SELECT knumv, kposn, stunr, kschl, kdatu, kherk, kinak
      FROM konv
      FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @IT_vbak
      WHERE knumv = @IT_vbak-knumv AND
            kdatu IN @s_kdatu AND
            kposn IN @s_kposn AND
            stunr IN @s_stunr AND
            kschl IN @s_kschl AND
            kherk IN @s_kherk AND
            kinak IN @s_kinak
      INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @IT_konv
      .

  ENDIF.

  " MOVE-CORRESPONDING it_vbak TO it_tab.
  FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs_vbak> TYPE ty_vbak.
  FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs_konv> TYPE ty_konv.
  FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs_tab_target> TYPE ty_tab.
  LOOP AT it_vbak ASSIGNING <fs_vbak>.
    LOOP AT it_konv ASSIGNING <fs_konv>
        WHERE knumv = <fs_vbak>-knumv.
      APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_tab ASSIGNING <fs_tab_target>.
      MOVE-CORRESPONDING <fs_vbak> to <fs_tab_target>.
      MOVE-CORRESPONDING <fs_konv> to <fs_tab_target>.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDLOOP.

entry.title: Re: SAPUI5 Meets TypeScript: A Smooth Migration Journey with Joule
entry.content:
Hi Marian,

Thanks for this detailed feedback and testing, this is very valuable feedback. There is context specific information which is being enriched during the process to convert UI5 controllers implemented in JavaScript to TypeScript. For the decision making, the more details are provided in the code, such as proper namespaces in the arguments and referenced modules in the documentation of the code, the better the results, as this information will be leveraged.

Regarding the version to be installed, the important information should be specified in the ui5.yaml in the framework section (https://sap.github.io/ui5-tooling/v3/pages/Configuration/) this configuration is being used to install the same version of the corresponding types for the installed UI5 version when using /ui5-typescript, falling back to the latest release if not provided as in your case.

We will constantly strive to improve the quality of the provided answers, taking your testing into account, this is very much appreciated.

Thank you & Best Regards

Martin
